speaker: interviewer
text: Ludwig Weiler in London. This is tape seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was about to say that the arrival in Birkenau. I missed a small point, , that as the train went through Hungary from west to east, it was the city of [? Koshal-- ?] or Kosice, it's called in Czechoslovakian. It was where the train was handed over by the Hungarian gendarme-- who conducted all the guarding and the pushing and shoving of the people on that, on the train-- to the SS, who as I mentioned in an earlier tape, consisted of this Muslim SS brigade from Bosnia. Now, it was on a Saturday morning approximately 9:00 o'clock when we arrived in Birkenau. And suddenly, doors are opened. The-- a lot of shouting. We, we didn't even comprehend exactly what was happening-- and out, out, raus, raus, raus, that-- which is in many films you have seen, no doubt. And we looked around, step out without anything. We didn't have a lot of belongings. We had nothing. And I'll never forget that I got off-- first of all, they separated men in one line, women next to it. And we faced back that way. So the ones who lived went to the left. And the ones who didn't went to the right. So it depended on the day which way they lined up the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to my father, I said-- don't forget I was 14 years and two, three months old-- and I was green to it all. I, I came from a comfortable home. And I didn't know where I was. And I said, but how will we exist here? I don't even have a toothbrush now. You know, it's strange-- this one story, how it stuck in my mind. So anyway, we got lined up. The families tried to sort of stay together. And I personally saw when the older brother of my father, Dr. Bela Weiler, who was the lawyer who I referred to a number of times, was sent to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father put himself out. He was, in 1944, 53 years old. He, I think, asked him how old you are. And my father said he's 49. Obviously, he must have already sensed that it's not good not to be in work. Because that was the whole story-- as long as you work, you're all right. So he sent him in the living direction. My brother, who was a 20-year-old-- there was no questions about him. So he went. And I came. And I was rela-- very quite fat, not very tall. But I was sort of broad in the shoulders. You can still see some of it today.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know how-- I just sort of stepped after them. And he did like that. I didn't even look at him. And I just ran after them, you know? So it could have been that he really didn't care. It could have been that he might have, for a second, looked in another direction. There was a German officer who was Mengele. I didn't know who he was. And, and my father went that way and brother, so I went that way. What's the difference?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we get into the camp. It was a hot day. We were sent into an area which is properly described in many documentations, which was the showers. They took all our clothing away, except the shoes. They left the shoes. And we were told that-- you got shaven off the hair and so on-- that we are going to have a shower, and so on. We [? really ?] believed. We had no reason [? not to. ?] And we had the shower. And then when we finished, we were lining up. Everybody got a packet, more or less the striped uniform with the cap, you know, the [? mitza ?], they called it. And you had your own shoes and some sort of underwear-- I can't even remember what it was. No, no, no-- there was no underwear. And that was it, I think. Anyway, all right-- we were lined up and taken into-- after the shower, into a barrack. That was unbelievable. It was a huge, big barrack, very little windows on it. And in the middle was like a brick-built-- like, a little oven-looking thing, but a low brick division, more or less, in the middle, if you like. It must have been about a meter wide and about 75 centimeters high. You could sit down on it, if you wanted, not that anybody would. They pushed into that barrack. It was [GERMAN SPEECH]. That means the gypsy camp. Because there were different numbers of camps. One was the [? A lager. ?] The gypsy camp was basically the worst, as such. Because the gypsies were totally cruel people. They were themselves deported. But the Germans realized that so amongst the Jews, amongst any others, they used the most brutal type of people to carry out being a kapo or blockalteste. Blockalteste means the guy who was in charge of the block. He was just as equally bad as, as the kapo. There were some nicer people and some not so nice. But the Germans knew the method to what sort of people to use for what sort of job. We got lined up in there. So in comes a German-speaking supervisor of the camp, being an inmate himself. And they were like little [INAUDIBLE]. You know, you had to be worried. I'm talking of later days, when we realized what the setup was. And he went on to say that we have got machines-- which, in those days, one didn't know-- where we can put an x-ray through your shoes. And if you've got any valuables there or anything, give it now. Because we will test every shoe. And if one is found, you will be hanged immediately. That was the [INAUDIBLE]. That was the [INAUDIBLE]. Some people maybe did, some didn't. I don't know. We, we didn't even have any with us. But then when that was finished, they said, now, dismissed, meaning the end of the, of the action. And that is where you're going to sleep-- a concrete floor. There was literally room to stand next to each other. You could hardly sit down. I mean, you have to squat to sit down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the first afternoon experience of what it was. The next thing is-- right. What does one need most than anything else? To go to the toilet. The order was that only 50 people can at one time go. And if there are 48, they have to wait for another two till they're ready to, to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: You were not allowed to go either individually, pairs, or fives, or tens to go into the latrine, which was the so-called toilet. It wasn't English flush toilets. OK-- we get used to that. Then, I don't think-- I can't remember if we got a little bit of bread or some rations that afternoon, certainly nothing warm. I mean, we were on the train a few days and all that. But, OK, that was nothing tragic.

speaker: interviewer
text: The next day, appell first thing in the morning, early morning-- what does that mean? You get lined up in groups of 10 wide and 20 deep, or whatever. Right. Counting, and counting, and counting, the never-ending job-- we stand there. And we don't know what's happening. OK, that's done. Then back to the barrack, because we were not working there. And then eventually was so-called lunch time, if you like, what the food was being. How did the food look like? You lined up again like in an appell. And one bowl of some watery, which was called [? durgem ?], you say, some floating bits of vegetable-- if you can call it vegetable, or imitation grass, I don't know what-- was given, a bowl in the hand of the guy who stood on the corner and no spoon, no fork, no. I mean, you know, one came from a normal existence. And you get dropped into the pits, you know? So how do you drink? How do you eat? You take it to your mouth and you drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some are older, some are less old, and whatnot. One is small. One is tall. One wants to drink. The other one is waiting. Give it to me, there won't be enough. And I mean, there was not a great deal of talking. So it was quite a commotion to get used to the feeding exercise, if you know what I mean. That was one thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other thing was that we-- really on the first day got to know when we had an opportunity to talk to anybody, and it went around like wildfire-- can you see the big fire and flame and smoke? That's where your relatives are going up now. We knew that literally within hours when we were there. That is when it hit us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, it must have terribly affected psychologically the people-- of fathers and mothers with children and brothers and sisters. And I must say that life is not fair, but that's how it is. The younger people like me, who was 14, and 15, and 16, even 18-- It has not left such a, a psychological scar on them, because a child is a child. You know, there is a tomorrow. You don't think with the mind of a grown-up. And, and you accept things. You have got to fall-- you fall. You get up-- you get up. Now when a person who, who knows every member of the family and is already at a mature age, this is a terrible experience. And I have seen people who ran against the electric barbed wire. That's what I'm trying to say. Anyway, the, the-- because they just couldn't take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we got to know the lager, the camp methods, the cruelties. And these were, in the main, carried out by inmates who were in charge, kapos and otherwise. First of all, we got to know what is a hand blow to the face. It was an interesting way how they did it. I mean, you know, afterwards in the camp, we practiced it on each other-- how it's done, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: You hit somebody under the chin. The guy who hits you has got firmly his hand here. And with one knock, he flattens you on the ground. It's amazing. You know, it is like a pilot learns how to land the plane. They were experienced how to immediately incapacitate people in minutes. There was a certain method.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what does one do? One learns how to avoid it. One becomes streetwise, if you know. One of the things was never volunteer. For instance, a day after our arrival, on the appel-- not the first day, the second day-- German officer comes along. And this-- the, the words are spoken by the blockalteste and the kapo. They didn't bother to talk to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, all people under the age of 16, step out of the line. Because you're going to go on easier work in a jam factory. I did not step out. Many did-- not everybody who were under 16. They were never seen daylight again. That was the last-- last day of appell that they had.

speaker: interviewee
text: So obviously, one learned never to volunteer. Never to stand on the front, not in the back, and not in the side-- always be in the middle somewhere. It is useful when you stand for hours on appell-- and I'm talking already of Buchenwald, later times. Because when it's very cold, if two people stand behind you, you don't get the freezing snow or the, or the wind on you. But somebody else gets it.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it is amazing how quickly a person can adopt to inhumane, unreasonable conditions and still say, fine. We will make it somehow. And at this point, I think I must explain that the survival of anybody depended on their luck-- [YIDDISH SPEECH,] as they say in Yiddish. And it was a matter of luck at what age you were deported, at which camp you got to-- you know, many other things. But there was a certain amount of things which you could do yourself. And the first and the foremost condition was never to give up. I have seen people later on, when I was in the other work camp in Ramsdorf, who were perfectly healthy looking people-- I mean, perfectly healthy in the circumstances, as people looked at the time. And once they started talking that we will never get out, we will get finished, this is the end, within a day or two, they were dead for no reason whatsoever. And there is a lesson to be learned in normal life, not only in unreasonable circumstances, that the, the point of survival is to never give up hope. Because you don't know what's around the corner. Something-- just as well as a bad thing can happen-- something unusually good can happen as well. And the people who gave up hope, they were finished and dead, gone. Now, there was another saying in the camps. They said that all the good people didn't survive. Maybe they were too nice. Maybe they didn't have the guts to, to, to, to-- you see, look, there were many. Some were not healthy. I mean, for instance, in Birkenau, they immediately, after the selection, a day or two after, they looked. If anybody had a hernia, they asked him to come out. you know, there were subsequent selections before the transport went away to a work camp. Anyhow, within five days, we were put together, a transport of 6,000 people. We were commandeered down to the railway line into the cattle trucks. And off we went. We got a certain amount of ration. I remember in early afternoon we left. And we happily ate our rations up the same day, thinking that, you know, after all, something we would get. We travelled two and a half days to Buchenwald, which is Turingia from the area of Auschwitz, Katowice, Silesia there, which Buchenwald is eight kilometers from Weimar in Turingia. We went through Dresden, Chemnitz. You know, we saw some of the stations. Not a morsel of food-- this was literally probably the longest time that we had not a drop of water or anything. And it wasn't really encouraging by the time we arrived. But on the main, those people survived. We arrived in Buchenwald. Buchenwald was not an extermination camp. Even so, it was the first German concentration camp from 1933. That was a very organized place. We, we got in. They, they took us through, again, the motion of showers, disinfection. And we got our lager numbers and a document, which I'm pleased to provide you to, to show. We were taken all our personal details-- our name, full name, year of birth, place, address, and the age, obviously, which is the date of birth, plus what languages one spoke, any unusual deformities, eyes color, nose, mouth, whatever you like-- comprehensive. Now, all these records were provided in forms, which you had to sign. And, and on the form-- can you imagine, after Birkenau-- It says that it is criminal to make a false declaration. And you will be punished for it. I mean, it seems a rather odd threat after you have been Birkenau and Auschwitz. However, one did take note of it. And there was-- but nevertheless-- and one had to sign it. Now, I told you that my father was born in 1891. He made his age 1895, four years older-- younger. My brother was born in 1924. He made himself younger to get closer to my age, 1926. And I made myself two years older, being 1928. And surprisingly enough, we managed to stay together, which was most unusual. Because the camp organizations were instructed to split brothers and fathers and children to break them apart. The events then after we were taken to the lower part of the camp, which was called [INAUDIBLE], which is the tent camp. Because there was a middle lager and a [? grosslager. ?] Buchenwald was a very large camp, was to very large extent by inmates of German political [INAUDIBLE]-- it means inmate-- Norwegians, French, Dutch, and Russian prisoners of war later, not at that time, Practically of all nationalities.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Jews-- you Know, everybody had a triangle of a color. Now, yellow was for Jews. That was very bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you had a black, you were a gypsy. If you had a green, you were a criminal. If you had a mauve triangle, you were homosexual. If you were a political prisoner, like the communists and socialists, it was a red triangle. Now, the, worst of it was if somebody had like a Magen David, which was a yellow triangle being a Jew and a cross with a red one, that he's a communist or socialist, a political plus a Jew. That was the bottom of the line. Anyway, I'm just trying to explain to you the certain marks which had. And from then on, you were not known by name, but by a number. Mine was 66,630-- 66,226. My father's was 25. And my brother's was 27. They tried to sandwich me in the middle of it, so if, if we ever get split, either I go with my father or I go with my brother. You know, because I was literally at a very young age. These are just the personal miniature events which took place. And within a few days, the transport of 6,000 people was broken up into two halves, be-- Buchenwald being a transit camp. And half of it was sent-- 3,000 people, or 2,800, 2,900, or something like that-- to Magdeburg, which was a very, very bad camp. And those were the people with surnames from A to K. Because in spite of the fact that everybody had a number, they had a record what their name was. And strange enough, that was the method how they split the transport-- not according to height or age or anything, just by the surname, A to K, Magdeburg, K to W, to [? Trieste. ?] [? Trieste ?] [INAUDIBLE], which later on became known by the name of Ramsdorf. I will explain to you later how that happened. And these were literally work camps, the typical camps where people got not too much food, a lot of long hours and hard work, and under quite inhumane conditions, where literally within months, people were finished. Now luckily also, my father, my brother and myself were amongst approximately 100 people who were over the quota. That means we were not sent to Magdeburg-- because our initial was W. And we got left. Now that, I would consider, was the least troublesome period of my camp life, being from 18th of July, I think, when we arrived in Buchenwald, till September. Now, a lot of things happened in that time. I had the advantage of speaking fluent German, that anything which went on was going on in German. Everybody had to-- even they mumble two words, the language was not any other language. And you were addressed in German. You had to answer in German. But on the other hand, this was a big plus for me, because I had no difficulty with that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we started off in the [INAUDIBLE] lager, the tent camp. And then eventually, somehow, we managed to [? will ?] ourselves into the main camp, where amongst all the blocks-- and there were some of them two stories high-- there was only one Jewish block, block 22. These were Jews and important prisoners of-- politically important prisoners of Jews who were taken to Buchenwald already years and years before from, from, from France, from Germany, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that summer of 1944, they made another block-- block number 23, a Jewish block. And we got in there. And I must say, life there was-- considering it was a concentration camp-- quite tolerable. It wasn't the worst. My father and my brother had to go out to work. And there was a [INAUDIBLE], which was a, a stone quarry. What they did there didn't build the German Reich. However, that was their occupation. I, a being a relatively young guy, [? wheedled ?] my way around in, in the block. And I used to do messages and carrying. So I did not go out daily to do the hard work. And maybe that also helped me that I didn't get so quickly weakened. The-- because There was also a, a little wood workshop in, in the camp. And sometime, I went in to work there. You know, I was sort of sent in between places, and I maneuver.

speaker: interviewee
text: But before we were taken-- before we got into that block 23, I remember in [INAUDIBLE], there was a Russian prisoner of war who was deaf mute-- deaf and dumb. And I sat on the ground, you know, during the day. And we were making marks in the sand with our finger. And first, we made a, a, a, [INAUDIBLE] a swastika. And we put a cross over it-- bad, you know? I couldn't speak Russian, he couldn't speak at all. And then we made a hammer and sickle-- mm, well. And this went on for about a half an hour or so. You know, it was just whiling away time. And suddenly, the guy takes out a [? seder. ?] Now, [? seder ?] was by sentence of death if they caught you with it. And he gave it to me. It was like as if I tell you that you have suddenly been given a jumbo jet, you know? It was out of the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: I put it in my pocket. It was a small [? seder. ?] So eventually, I took that with me. They never found it on me. There was no body searches, if you know what I mean. And we were not taken regularly for disinfection or, or, or change of clothing or anything. So nobody bothered, really. But anyway, we got into the block 23. And I must say that, maybe understandably, the main part of the occupants, of the other inmates, were young or not so young-- some of them boys-- who originated from the Warsaw ghetto and jumped off the trains to Treblinka but were caught later on. And they were obviously not taken to Treblinka. Maybe by the time it was 1944, and the Warsaw ghetto was liquidated already two, two and a half years earlier, and came to Buchenwald. Now I understand-- and I'm not having any arguments about it. Because, you see, what one should not forget-- there was not an absolute harmony in these camps. There were plenty of in-fights. There was plenty of terrible things which certain people did to others for no reason at all-- just nastiness, you know-- which can, could cost them their lives. And these didn't have to be kapos or blockaltestes. Human nature, anyway-- even amongst our own people. Let's be quite frank about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these boys obviously were already five years under terrible conditions. And without exception, whatever Hasidic or religious background they came from, they were practically hated anything religious. Don't forget, we came out four months earlier. What do you mean, four months earlier? Three months earlier, we went to shul regularly from on the Pesach, So for me, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And they got to know-- first, they didn't like us right away. Maybe because we were Hungarian Jews. But that's beside the point. We didn't speak Yiddish. We spoke German. And there was a somewhat tense atmosphere, insofar that we were not gladly accepted. Even so, we were there. But nothing terrible happened, except that they knew that we were religious. They found out I had the [? seder. ?] And they went bonkers. They, they hated us after that. They didn't do anything. They didn't report me, or anything-- nothing. However, in September of 1944, when the Riga ghetto was liquidated, they brought 1,500 people back, men. And a transport was organized. And of course, the block-- the people who were in charge of individual block, being Jews there, inmates, they had the power to delegate whether you do this work, or whether you go into a transport, or you don't go into it. Now, to go out of Buchenwald was not exactly helpful. Because it was a relatively, as a concentration camp goes-- because it was mainly inhabited by non-Jews, the regime for Jews, even in the concentration camp, was worse than for non-Jews. There was a distinct difference drawn. And even some of the inmates-- might as well-- they may have been caught for some other reason. They were criminals, or something. So it wasn't all we are united. Everybody who has got a prison uniform is the same. And we all are brothers. No, it wasn't. And this Jewish management pushed us into the transport with these Jews, very nice people from Riga ghetto who came. And we were sent out to [INAUDIBLE], to the same place where our original half transport went. Because the first 1,500 people, they were already sent back from-- as a [INAUDIBLE] transport to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that is when we arrived in-- it was called [INAUDIBLE]. It's near Erfurt, eastern Germany. And it was a camp on the lower part of the side of a hill. And that was the [? summer ?] camp.
